quiz_questions.question_text: Which group on the periodic table contains the alkali metals?

options.text: Group 2
Group 1
Group 17
Group 18

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals are in Group 1 of the periodic table (excluding hydrogen). They include lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, cesium, and francium.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many valence electrons do alkali metals have?

options.text: 2 valence electrons
7 valence electrons
1 valence electron
8 valence electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals have 1 valence electron. This single electron is easily lost, which is why alkali metals are extremely reactive and form +1 ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when alkali metals are placed in water?

options.text: They sink to the bottom and nothing happens at all
They react vigorously, often producing hydrogen gas
They dissolve slowly without any visible change or reaction
They freeze the water around them into solid ice crystals

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals react vigorously with water, producing hydrogen gas and a metal hydroxide. The reaction can be explosive with larger alkali metals like potassium and cesium.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which group contains the alkaline earth metals?

options.text: Group 1
Group 2
Group 12
Group 17

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkaline earth metals are in Group 2 of the periodic table. They include beryllium, magnesium, calcium, strontium, barium, and radium.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many valence electrons do alkaline earth metals have?

options.text: 1 valence electron
2 valence electrons
3 valence electrons
4 valence electrons

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkaline earth metals have 2 valence electrons. They tend to lose both electrons when forming compounds, creating +2 ions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Are alkali metals more or less reactive than alkaline earth metals?

options.text: Less reactive than alkaline earth metals
More reactive than alkaline earth metals
Exactly the same reactivity as the others
Neither group is reactive with anything

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals (Group 1) are generally more reactive than alkaline earth metals (Group 2) because they have only 1 valence electron to lose, making it easier to react.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why must alkali metals be stored in oil or under an inert atmosphere?

options.text: To keep them warm and prevent them from freezing
To prevent them from reacting with air and moisture
To make them more reactive when they are needed
To change their color so they look more attractive

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals are so reactive that they will quickly react with oxygen and water vapor in the air. Storing them in oil or under argon gas prevents unwanted reactions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which alkaline earth metal is found in bones and teeth?

options.text: Magnesium
Beryllium
Barium
Calcium

quiz_questions.explanation: Calcium is an essential alkaline earth metal found in bones and teeth as calcium phosphate. It is the most abundant metal in the human body.

quiz_questions.question_text: What charge do alkali metal ions typically have?

options.text: A +2 charge
A -1 charge
A +1 charge
A -2 charge

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals form +1 ions by losing their single valence electron. For example, sodium (Na) loses one electron to become Na+.

quiz_questions.question_text: As you go down Group 1 from lithium to francium, what happens to reactivity?

options.text: Reactivity stays the same
Reactivity decreases
Reactivity increases
Reactivity first goes up then down

quiz_questions.explanation: Reactivity increases going down Group 1 because the valence electron is farther from the nucleus and easier to remove. Francium is the most reactive alkali metal.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an alkali metal?

options.text: A highly reactive metal in Group 1 with one valence electron
A nonreactive gas found in Group 18 of the periodic table
A metalloid element found along the staircase line on the table
A transition metal found in Groups 3 through 12 of the table

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals are the highly reactive metals in Group 1 of the periodic table. They have 1 valence electron, are soft, have low density, and react vigorously with water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an alkaline earth metal?

options.text: A reactive metal in Group 2 with two valence electrons total
A nonmetal element found in Group 16 of the periodic table
A noble gas found in the last column of the periodic table
A halogen element found in Group 17 of the periodic table

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkaline earth metals are reactive metals in Group 2 with 2 valence electrons. They are harder and denser than alkali metals but still quite reactive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'soft metal' mean when describing alkali metals?

options.text: They can be easily cut with a knife due to weak metallic bonds
They dissolve in water and lose their solid form right away
They are flexible like rubber and can stretch without breaking
They exist as liquids at standard room temperature and pressure

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals are described as soft because they can be easily cut with a knife or fingernail. This is due to their relatively weak metallic bonds compared to other metals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'low density' mean for alkali metals?

options.text: They have less mass per unit volume and some can float on water
They are extremely heavy and always sink to the bottom of water
They take up a very large amount of space compared to other metals
They cannot be measured on a balance because they weigh too little

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals have low density compared to most other metals. Lithium, sodium, and potassium are less dense than water and would float on it if they did not react with it so vigorously.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a cation?

options.text: A positively charged ion formed when an atom loses electrons
A negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains electrons
A neutral atom that has not gained or lost any electrons yet
A subatomic particle located in the nucleus of every atom

quiz_questions.explanation: A cation is a positively charged ion formed when an atom loses one or more electrons. Metals like alkali and alkaline earth metals form cations because they easily lose valence electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'hydroxide' mean?

options.text: A compound or ion containing oxygen and hydrogen with a negative charge
A type of metal that is found only in Group 1 of the periodic table today
A solution that is always acidic and has a pH lower than the value of 7
A gas that is released when nonmetals are heated to very high temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: A hydroxide is a polyatomic ion (OH-) containing one oxygen and one hydrogen atom with a negative charge. When alkali metals react with water, they form metal hydroxides (like NaOH).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'flame test' mean in chemistry?

options.text: A test where a substance is burned to identify it by the flame color produced
A test to determine how quickly a substance will catch fire in open air today
A test that measures the exact temperature at which a substance melts down
A test that determines whether a substance is a metal or a nonmetal element

quiz_questions.explanation: A flame test is a procedure where a substance is held in a flame to observe the characteristic color it produces. Different metals produce different colors: sodium gives yellow, potassium gives violet, and lithium gives red.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an ion?

options.text: An atom that has gained or lost electrons and carries a charge
A neutral atom with equal numbers of protons and electrons
A molecule made of two or more different types of atoms bonded
A subatomic particle that has no electrical charge whatsoever

quiz_questions.explanation: An ion is an atom or group of atoms that has gained or lost electrons, giving it a net electrical charge. Positive ions are cations and negative ions are anions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'oxidation' mean when describing alkali metals?

options.text: The process of losing electrons during a chemical reaction with another substance
The process of gaining electrons during a chemical reaction with another substance
The process of melting a metal by heating it to extremely high temperatures in a lab
The process of mixing two metals together to create a new and stronger alloy mixture

quiz_questions.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons. Alkali metals are easily oxidized because they readily lose their single valence electron. When exposed to air, they quickly form a dull oxide layer on their surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'electrolyte' mean?

options.text: A substance that dissolves in water to form a solution that conducts electricity
A type of battery that uses only alkali metals to generate electrical power
An insulator that prevents electricity from flowing through any water solution
A device used in laboratories to measure the electrical charge of metal ions

quiz_questions.explanation: An electrolyte is a substance that produces ions when dissolved in water, allowing the solution to conduct electricity. Many alkali and alkaline earth metal compounds are strong electrolytes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a small piece of sodium in water and observes it fizzing, moving rapidly across the surface, and producing a gas. What gas is being produced?

options.text: Oxygen gas from the breakdown of the water molecules
Hydrogen gas from the reaction of sodium with water
Carbon dioxide gas from sodium reacting with the air
Nitrogen gas from the decomposition of the sodium metal

quiz_questions.explanation: When sodium reacts with water, it produces sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrogen gas (H2). The reaction is: 2Na + 2H2O -> 2NaOH + H2. The fizzing is caused by hydrogen gas bubbles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fireworks produce different colors because they contain different metal salts. Sodium produces yellow light and strontium produces red light. What technique is this similar to in a chemistry lab?

options.text: Water displacement testing
The flame test technique
Density calculations method
The litmus paper testing

quiz_questions.explanation: This is the same principle as a flame test, where different metals produce characteristic colors when heated. Fireworks use this property to create colorful displays.

quiz_questions.question_text: Calcium is added to many foods and supplements for bone health. As an alkaline earth metal, what charge does calcium have when it forms an ion?

options.text: +1, because it loses one electron from its outer shell
+2, because it loses two electrons from its outer shell
-1, because it gains one electron to fill its outer shell
-2, because it gains two electrons to fill its outer shell

quiz_questions.explanation: As a Group 2 element, calcium has 2 valence electrons. It loses both to form a Ca2+ ion with a +2 charge, achieving a stable electron configuration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Potassium (K) is more reactive with water than sodium (Na). Both are alkali metals, but potassium is lower in Group 1. Why is potassium more reactive?

options.text: Potassium has more valence electrons than sodium does overall
Potassium is smaller than sodium so its electrons are held more tightly
Potassium is larger so its valence electron is farther from the nucleus
Potassium has fewer protons so its nucleus pulls electrons less strongly

quiz_questions.explanation: Potassium has more electron shells than sodium, so its single valence electron is farther from the nucleus and less tightly held. This makes it easier to lose, increasing reactivity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Table salt (NaCl) is made from sodium, a dangerously reactive metal, and chlorine, a toxic gas. Why is table salt safe to eat?

options.text: The sodium and chlorine atoms are still separate in salt and do not interact
When they combine chemically, a new stable compound forms with different properties
Salt contains so little sodium and chlorine that their dangerous effects are gone
The chlorine evaporates during the mixing process leaving only harmless sodium behind

quiz_questions.explanation: When sodium and chlorine combine in a chemical reaction, they form sodium chloride (NaCl), a completely new compound with different properties from either element alone. Compounds often have very different properties than their component elements.

quiz_questions.question_text: Magnesium is used in lightweight alloys for aircraft. Which properties of this alkaline earth metal make it useful for this purpose?

options.text: High reactivity and soft texture make it easy to apply to aircraft
Low density and good strength make it ideal for lightweight aircraft
Poor conductivity and brittleness make it safe for aircraft engines
High melting point and radioactivity make it useful for aircraft fuel

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnesium's low density (it is the lightest structural metal) combined with good strength makes it ideal for aircraft where reducing weight is critical for fuel efficiency and performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student cuts a piece of lithium with a knife and notices the freshly cut surface is shiny, but it quickly turns dull. What is happening?

options.text: The lithium is melting due to the heat from the knife used to cut it
The lithium is reacting with oxygen in the air forming an oxide layer
The lithium is absorbing light which makes it appear darker in color
The lithium is dissolving in the moisture present in the student's hand

quiz_questions.explanation: Alkali metals like lithium are so reactive that they immediately react with oxygen in the air, forming a dull oxide coating on the surface. This is why freshly cut alkali metals quickly lose their shine.

quiz_questions.question_text: Barium compounds are used in medical imaging for X-rays of the digestive system. Barium is an alkaline earth metal in Group 2. How many valence electrons does barium have?

options.text: 1 valence electron, like all metals in the first two groups
2 valence electrons, like all other Group 2 elements have
6 valence electrons, because barium is in the sixth period
8 valence electrons, because barium has a stable outer shell

quiz_questions.explanation: As a Group 2 element, barium has 2 valence electrons, just like all other alkaline earth metals. The group number for main group elements tells you the number of valence electrons.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sodium is used in streetlights because it produces a bright yellow-orange glow. Sodium is in Group 1 and Period 3. Is sodium more or less reactive than cesium (Group 1, Period 6)?

options.text: More reactive, because sodium is lighter and smaller than cesium
Less reactive, because cesium is lower in Group 1 than sodium is
Equally reactive, because both are in Group 1 of the periodic table
Less reactive, because sodium has fewer protons than cesium does

quiz_questions.explanation: Sodium is less reactive than cesium. Reactivity increases going down Group 1 because larger atoms have their valence electron farther from the nucleus, making it easier to lose.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student knows that calcium reacts with water but less vigorously than sodium does. The student predicts that magnesium (above calcium in Group 2) would react even less vigorously with water. Is this prediction correct?

options.text: No, magnesium should react more vigorously since it is higher in Group 2
No, all alkaline earth metals react with water at the exact same intensity level
Yes, elements higher in a group are less reactive so magnesium reacts less
Yes, but only because magnesium has more valence electrons than calcium does

quiz_questions.explanation: The prediction is correct. For metals, reactivity increases going down a group. Magnesium is above calcium in Group 2, so it is less reactive. Magnesium reacts very slowly with cold water.
